stages.name: unit_tests
stages.evidence: test/integration_run_test.zig spawns the BUILT zentinel binary against the committed fixture project test/fixtures/integration/sample (copied into an isolated tmp dir), captures the canonical JSON report from stdout, and asserts total=2, killed=1, survived=1 -- so the real src/cli.zig adapters (std.process.run execProcess, setupWorkspace tree-copy, JSON report writing) run under `zig build test`. Before the build harness existed the test failed to compile ('no module named integration_options'); after wiring it passes (500 tests). A regression demonstration confirmed it is not a tautology: temporarily covering mul made the real binary report killed=2/survived=0 and the test failed ('expected 1, found 2').

stages.name: mutation_checks
stages.reason: task 111 adds an integration test, a fixture project, and build wiring (src/** is not in the allowed files and was not changed); the new coverage is itself proven by a test that fails (no harness) without the wiring and catches a real-binary classification change.

residual_risk: The integration test spawns the built binary which spawns nested `zig test calc.zig` per mutant, so it is slower than the mock-executor unit tests and depends on `zig` being on PATH in the CI environment (the same `zig` that invoked `zig build test`); it uses an isolated tmp copy of the fixture and `tmp.cleanup()`, so it never writes into the committed tree.

It asserts the summary counts (total/killed/survived) rather than full report bytes, to stay robust to volatile observation metadata (run id, timestamps, durations) without re-implementing normalization in the test; the counts are derived from the real binary's stdout, so a regression in execProcess/setupWorkspace/report-writing that changes classification is caught.

The binary path is passed build-root-relative via addOptionPath and joined with the absolute root_dir option; if a future build emits an already-absolute path the test handles it via std.fs.path.isAbsolute.
